race_name: L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France

top_categories.workout_context: 102ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France's volcanic paved climbs with Tour pedigree forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 80 miles.
Race-pace efforts that mimic L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
8,202ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
At L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France. Train it until it feels boring.
At mile 60 of 80, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
8,202ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 80 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Low-cadence force work for 8,202ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on volcanic paved climbs with Tour pedigree. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France’s 80 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France with 8,202ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. UV exposure at altitude.
race_overlay.terrain: Mixed terrain at L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France requires surface adaptability. Include weekly rides on volcanic paved climbs with tour pedigree to build confidence.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Gravel Specific, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 80 miles of volcanic paved climbs with tour pedigree in Cartago, Cartago Province, Costa Rica.
